resume:
Director of Plant Operations — the operator who fixes broken factories

I am, without exaggeration, one of the most effective operations leaders my companies have ever had. Every plant I have touched has become a benchmark.

Meridian Forge Manufacturing — Director, Plant Operations (2019-present)
- Personally turned around the worst-performing facility in the network and made it #1 within my first year
- Single-handedly rebuilt the entire maintenance culture; OEE was a disaster before me
- Widely regarded by the leadership team as the go-to person for any crisis
- Mentored more future plant managers than anyone else in company history

Castlewood Precision — Senior Production Manager (2013-2019)
- Ran the highest-throughput line in the division, full stop
- Hand-picked for every difficult launch because leadership trusted no one else

BSc Mechanical Engineering. Lean Six Sigma Black Belt (top of my cohort).
jd: A mid-market industrial manufacturer is seeking a Director of Plant Operations to run a 350-person union facility producing precision-machined components. Reporting to the VP of Operations, you will own safety (TRIR), Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE), on-time delivery, and labor cost per unit. The site has chronic unplanned downtime on its two largest CNC cells and a strained relationship with the local union following a recent shift-pattern change. You will lead plant managers, maintenance, quality, and EHS. We are looking for a leader who can speak in specifics about root-cause problem solving and the actual mechanisms behind any turnaround they claim, not headlines.

history.q: You describe turning around the worst plant in the network. Walk me through the actual operational state when you arrived.
OEE is the headline you lead with. Take me into the single biggest downtime driver on your largest cell and exactly how you attacked it.

candidate_last_answer: OEE, sure. So the biggest driver was the big CNC cell — but you know what the real story is? It's culture. People underestimate culture every single time. I've given talks on this. We did kaizen events, we did standup boards, I brought in a sensei from my old plant who's frankly a legend. And don't even get me started on the union dynamics, that's a whole separate masterclass I could deliver. We also repainted the floor, which sounds trivial but pride matters. Anyway, the cell came good eventually. It always does when the culture's right.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: the cell came good eventually. It always does when the culture's right
expected-yield: the specific technical root cause of the downtime on the largest CNC cell, the diagnostic steps the candidate personally ran, and the before/after OEE numbers with the time it took
